frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Arey beta, after marriage thoda sindoor ya mangalsutra pehenne se aurat settled lagti hai, suhagan ki pehchaan hoti hai.
B: But some girls don’t like wearing it daily.
A: Choice unki hai, par hamaari parampara ka bhi toh matlab hota hai. Aajkal ki ladkiyan sab modern ho gayi hain, par shaadi ke baad yeh chhoti chhoti cheezein rakhne se hi ghar-grihasthi ka maan bana rehta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as natural tradition and a married woman’s proper identity rather than control. It encodes completeness/settledness framing and disapproval of women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra regularly.
persona_text: You are Sunita. Age 48. Married for 24 years. A woman who worked for six years before stopping when her first child was born. You are at a family function sitting with three other women your age. One woman mentions that her daughter in law has just gone back to work full time when her child is only eight months old. The conversation opens up. You are giving your opinion on women who return to work this early and what it means for the child and what you think women of this generation have confused about what ambition is supposed to look like and what you gave up and whether you regret it.
